sections.heading: Why Vocabulary Matters
sections.body:
The average English speaker knows 20,000-35,000 word families. You need:
• 1,000 words for basic daily conversation
• 3,000 words for everyday reading (newspapers)
• 5,000+ words for professional/academic use

More vocabulary = more precise expression = better communication.

sections.heading: 5 Techniques to Learn Words
sections.body:
1. Word families: learn word + its forms
   decide → decision → decisive → indecisive
   help → helpful → helpless → helper

2. Collocations: words that go together
   make a decision (not 'do a decision')
   heavy rain (not 'strong rain')
   deep sleep (not 'big sleep')

3. Context clues: guess meaning from the sentence
   'The arid desert had no water for miles'
   Arid = dry (the context tells you)

4. Prefixes/Suffixes: break words into parts
   un- = not (unhappy, unable, unusual)
   -tion = noun (education, decision)
   -ly = adverb (quickly, carefully)

5. Spaced repetition: review at increasing intervals
   Day 1 → Day 3 → Day 7 → Day 14 → Day 30

sections.heading: Essential Word Groups
sections.body:
Transition words:
However, therefore, moreover, furthermore, additionally, nevertheless, consequently

Describing people:
Ambitious, reliable, creative, resourceful, empathetic, resilient, charismatic

Work vocabulary:
Deadline, milestone, collaborate, delegate, prioritize, implement, evaluate

jargon.definition: A group of words derived from the same root. Example: create, creation, creative, creatively, recreate.
Words that naturally go together in English. Example: 'make a mistake' (not 'do a mistake'), 'heavy traffic' (not 'strong traffic').
A group of letters added before a word to change its meaning. Examples: un- (not), re- (again), pre- (before), dis- (opposite).
A group of letters added after a word root to change its form or meaning. Examples: -tion (noun), -ly (adverb), -able (adjective).

summary: Build vocabulary strategically: learn word families, collocations, and use spaced repetition. Focus on high-frequency words first — 3,000 words cover 95% of everyday English.
